title: Tumor Lysis Syndrome in Acute Leukemia
subtitle: Oncologic Emergency & the Calcium-Phosphate Precipitation Trap

patient.name: T. Brooks
patient.admitDate: 5 days ago for newly diagnosed B-cell ALL; Day 2 post-induction chemo

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 108 with peaked T-waves V2-V4, widened QRS to 124 ms, prolonged QTc 498 ms, frequent PVCs and couplets
labs.r: Sinus rhythm without ischemic or electrolyte changes

nursesNote: 0600 — 24 y/o male admitted 5 days ago with newly diagnosed B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL). Presenting WBC was 92,000 with 88% blasts, bulky cervical/axillary lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly on CT — classified by oncology as HIGH TLS risk per Cairo-Bishop stratification. Pre-chemo prophylaxis included aggressive IV hydration (0.9% NS at 200 mL/hr) starting 24 hrs before chemo and allopurinol 300 mg PO daily started 12 hrs pre-chemo. Rasburicase was deferred at the time due to cost (G6PD status was unknown). Induction regimen (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, daunorubicin, prednisone) administered yesterday morning. Overnight, night shift reports progressive decrease in UOP (140 mL over last 8 hrs), new muscle cramps in calves, perioral numbness, increasing fatigue, and lethargy. Family at bedside reports client 'doesn't seem himself — drowsier, complaining his legs feel heavy.' On assessment: client awake but lethargic, oriented to person and place but slowed in responses; not consistently oriented to day. Positive Chvostek sign on facial nerve tapping. Positive Trousseau sign with BP cuff inflation for 90 seconds. DTRs brisk 3+ at biceps and patellar. Cardiac monitor: sinus rhythm 108 with frequent PVCs and occasional couplets; ECG strip review with peaked T-waves and widened QRS. Indwelling Foley draining dark amber concentrated urine; output 18 mL in the last hour. Diffuse petechiae over lower extremities (thrombocytopenia). Hickman central line site clean, intact, no erythema or drainage. Lung sounds clear. Bowel sounds present, no abdominal pain.
providerOrders: ICU transfer; oncology and nephrology STAT consults
Continuous cardiac monitoring with frequent ECG strip review
STAT BMP, phosphorus, ionized calcium, magnesium, uric acid, LDH, ABG, troponin, CBC
Increase IV hydration: 0.9% NS at 250 mL/hr (target UOP >100 mL/hr or ~2 mL/kg/hr)
Calcium gluconate 1 g (10 mL of 10% solution) IV slowly over 10 min — for cardiac membrane stabilization given hyperkalemic ECG changes WITH symptomatic hypocalcemia (Chvostek/Trousseau, PVCs)
Regular insulin 10 units IV PUSH + D50W 25 g IV PUSH simultaneously — shift K+ intracellularly
Albuterol 10–20 mg nebulized — adjunctive K+ shift
Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) 30 g PO/PR OR sodium zirconium 10 g PO if available
Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV x1 — urgent uric acid catabolism for established TLS with AKI (verify G6PD status before administration; G6PD deficiency = absolute contraindication)
Aluminum hydroxide 30 mL PO q4h with meals — phosphate binder
DO NOT routinely administer IV calcium for asymptomatic hypocalcemia (calcium-phosphate precipitation worsens AKI)
DO NOT administer LR or other potassium-containing fluids
DO NOT administer loop diuretics (will dehydrate and worsen urate precipitation)
Bicarbonate routinely AVOIDED (shifts equilibrium toward calcium-phosphate precipitation)
Repeat BMP, phosphorus, uric acid, ionized calcium q4h until stable
Strict I&O, hourly UOP, daily weights
Neutropenic precautions: private room, mask for visitors, no fresh flowers, no fresh fruits or vegetables, meticulous hand hygiene
Prepare for possible emergent CRRT if no response to medical therapy

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Potassium 6.4 mEq/L with peaked T-waves, widened QRS, and frequent PVCs on cardiac monitor
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — most immediately life-threatening derangement in TLS. Hyperkalemia with ECG changes is a medical emergency. Untreated, can progress within minutes to ventricular tachycardia, fibrillation, or asystole. The combination of K+ >6 PLUS ECG changes mandates urgent membrane stabilization with calcium and intracellular K+ shifting.

opts.text: Phosphorus 8.4 mg/dL with concurrent calcium 6.8 mg/dL (total) and ionized 0.92
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines the central physiologic problem in TLS. Hyperphosphatemia drives hypocalcemia (calcium binds phosphate stoichiometrically). The calcium-phosphate product (calcium x phosphorus = 57 here) >55 promotes precipitation of CaPO4 crystals in renal tubules, worsening AKI.

opts.text: Uric acid 14.8 mg/dL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Massive cell lysis releases nucleic acids that are catabolized through xanthine to uric acid. In acidic urine, uric acid crystallizes in renal tubules, causing urate nephropathy. Levels >10 mg/dL are associated with rapidly progressive AKI. Rasburicase rapidly catabolizes uric acid to allantoin (water-soluble); allopurinol blocks formation upstream but doesn't remove existing uric acid.

opts.text: Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (up from baseline 0.9, ~3x increase) with UOP 18 mL/hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — establishes clinical TLS (Cairo-Bishop). Lab TLS = electrolyte derangements alone; clinical TLS = lab TLS plus AKI (Cr ≥1.5x baseline), arrhythmia, or seizure. This client meets criteria for clinical TLS, which significantly worsens prognosis and accelerates the timeline for intervention including possible CRRT.

opts.text: Positive Chvostek sign and Trousseau sign with perioral numbness and muscle cramps
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — clinical hypocalcemia (neuromuscular irritability). Chvostek = facial nerve tap producing twitch; Trousseau = carpopedal spasm with BP cuff inflation. Severe hypocalcemia can progress to laryngospasm, tetany, and seizures. Note these are SYMPTOMATIC, which changes the calcium replacement decision (vs. asymptomatic where calcium should be withheld).

opts.text: LDH 1,840 U/L (~6x normal)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — biomarker of cell turnover. Markedly elevated LDH confirms massive ongoing tumor cell lysis. Trending LDH downward indicates response to chemo and resolution of the lysis burden; persistently rising LDH suggests ongoing or refractory disease.

opts.text: WBC 0.4 K/µL with ANC 120 (severe neutropenia)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — concurrent risk requiring strict precautions. ANC <500 cells/µL = severe neutropenia; any fever (≥100.4°F) in this state is a medical emergency (febrile neutropenia) requiring blood cultures + empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour. Note neutropenia is from chemo myelosuppression, separate from TLS itself.

opts.text: Initial presenting WBC 92,000 with 88% blasts, bulky lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly (HIGH TLS risk on admission)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — explains preventability. High tumor burden in hematologic malignancies is the strongest TLS risk factor. This client should have received rasburicase prophylactically, not just allopurinol. Recognizing this informs the family discussion and the urgency of current intervention.

opts.text: Lethargy and slowed responses with new disorientation to day
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. New mental status change in TLS can be from uremic encephalopathy (BUN 48), severe hypocalcemia affecting CNS, or evolving metabolic acidosis (pH 7.30). Trending mentation is a sensitive marker of overall metabolic status.

opts.text: Petechiae over lower extremities
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sign of chemo-induced thrombocytopenia (platelets 38). Indicates bleeding risk; avoid IM injections, use small-gauge needles, hold pressure longer, avoid NSAIDs/aspirin. Platelet transfusion threshold is typically <10 K/µL (prophylactic) or <50 K/µL with active bleeding or procedure planned.

opts.text: Bowel sounds present, no abdominal pain
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue — reassuring finding ruling out competing GI process (e.g., neutropenic enterocolitis/typhlitis, which can occur in this population).

opts.text: Hickman central line site clean and intact
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue — reassuring finding ruling out central-line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI), a major concern in neutropenic clients with indwelling devices. Document, no action needed.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Massive tumor cell lysis from cytotoxic chemotherapy is releasing intracellular contents (K+, phosphorus, nucleic acids) into the bloodstream faster than the kidneys can excrete them
opts.rat: CORRECT — defining mechanism. Highly chemosensitive tumors (ALL, Burkitt lymphoma, high-grade NHL) and bulky disease release massive amounts of intracellular contents when killed. The classic timing is 24–72 hours post-chemo for most regimens.

opts.text: Hyperphosphatemia drives hypocalcemia because phosphate binds calcium stoichiometrically, and calcium-phosphate crystals can precipitate in renal tubules worsening AKI
opts.rat: CORRECT — the central physiologic linkage. The product Ca x P >55–70 (mg²/dL²) puts the client at high risk of crystal precipitation. This is why replacing calcium in asymptomatic clients is HARMFUL — it pushes the equilibrium further toward precipitation and accelerates AKI.

opts.text: Nucleic acids from lysed cells are catabolized to uric acid, which precipitates in acidic renal tubules causing urate nephropathy
opts.rat: CORRECT. Purines → hypoxanthine → xanthine → uric acid (via xanthine oxidase). Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase (prevents new uric acid formation). Rasburicase enzymatically converts existing uric acid to water-soluble allantoin. Rasburicase is dramatically faster (hours vs. days).

opts.text: Cairo-Bishop criteria: this client meets clinical TLS = lab TLS (≥2 derangements) PLUS AKI (Cr ≥1.5x baseline), arrhythmia (PVCs), or seizure
opts.rat: CORRECT. Lab TLS criteria require ≥2 of: uric acid ≥8 mg/dL or 25% rise, K+ ≥6.0 or 25% rise, phosphate ≥4.5 (adult) or 25% rise, calcium ≤7 or 25% drop. This client has all four lab criteria PLUS AKI and PVCs — definite clinical TLS, the more severe form.

opts.text: The hyperkalemia is the most immediately life-threatening derangement and is treated with calcium (membrane stabilization), insulin/glucose (intracellular shift), and Kayexalate (elimination)
opts.rat: CORRECT. The 'CBIG-K' framework: Calcium (stabilize), Beta-agonist (shift), Insulin/D50 (shift), Glucose (with insulin), Kayexalate/sodium zirconium (eliminate). Dialysis is the definitive treatment for refractory hyperkalemia or persistent AKI.

opts.text: The neutropenia is part of TLS and will resolve with treatment of the metabolic derangements
opts.rat: INCORRECT. Neutropenia in this client is from chemo-induced myelosuppression, NOT TLS. It will resolve as the bone marrow recovers (typically 10–14 days post-induction with G-CSF support). Neutropenic precautions and prompt sepsis recognition are needed regardless of TLS resolution.

opts.text: IV calcium gluconate should be routinely administered to correct the hypocalcemia regardless of symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — HIGH-YIELD NCLEX TRAP. Calcium replacement in TLS is DANGEROUS in asymptomatic clients because it accelerates calcium-phosphate precipitation and worsens AKI. Reserved for: (1) symptomatic tetany/seizure, OR (2) hyperkalemic ECG changes requiring cardiac membrane stabilization. This client qualifies under both, so calcium IS given — but the principle is selective use.

opts.text: Furosemide should be given to increase urine output and 'flush out' the uric acid
opts.rat: INCORRECT. Loop diuretics are not first-line in TLS. They can volume-deplete the client (worsening urate precipitation in concentrated urine) and have not shown benefit in randomized data. Aggressive IV hydration alone is sufficient unless there's volume overload from oliguric AKI.

opts.text: Routine sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize the urine and prevent uric acid crystallization is a current standard practice
opts.rat: INCORRECT — outdated. Older protocols recommended urinary alkalinization, but this shifts the equilibrium toward calcium-phosphate crystal precipitation, worsening AKI. Modern guidelines (since the rasburicase era) recommend AGAINST routine alkalinization. Reserve bicarbonate for severe metabolic acidosis with pH <7.20 and hemodynamic instability.

opts.text: Lactated Ringers would be an appropriate IV fluid choice for aggressive hydration
opts.rat: INCORRECT. LR contains 4 mEq/L of potassium — contraindicated in hyperkalemia. Use 0.9% NS or other potassium-free crystalloid. This is a common pitfall when sepsis protocols are reflexively applied to TLS.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Arrhythmia r/t severe hyperkalemia and concurrent hypocalcemia
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Circulation (ABCs). Already evidenced by PVCs, couplets, peaked T-waves, and widened QRS. The combination of high K+ and low Ca is particularly arrhythmogenic. Untreated, can progress to VT/VF or asystole within minutes.

opts.text: Risk for Worsening AKI Requiring Dialysis r/t urate nephropathy and calcium-phosphate precipitation
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Already in stage 3 AKI (Cr ~3x baseline) with oliguria. Without aggressive intervention, will progress to anuric renal failure requiring CRRT. This drives the urgency of rasburicase, hydration, and phosphate binders.

opts.text: Risk for Seizure / Tetany r/t severe symptomatic hypocalcemia
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Chvostek and Trousseau are positive; further decline can produce carpopedal spasm, laryngospasm, or generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Resolves with calcium administration (which is indicated here because symptomatic) AND with treatment of the underlying hyperphosphatemia.

opts.text: Risk for Infection (Septic Shock) r/t severe neutropenia (ANC 120) from chemo-induced myelosuppression
opts.rat: #4 — Real and serious but distinct from the TLS emergency. Any fever ≥100.4°F is a medical emergency requiring blood cultures and empiric antibiotics within 1 hour. Currently afebrile, but neutropenic precautions must be maintained.

opts.text: Anxiety / Ineffective Coping r/t new cancer diagnosis and acute critical illness
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment, but real. New cancer diagnosis + complication is a profound psychological burden for the client and family. Calm presence, brief explanations of what's happening, social work and chaplaincy referrals are concurrent supportive measures.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly over 10 minutes for cardiac membrane stabilization given hyperkalemic ECG changes WITH symptomatic hypocalcemia
opts.rat: EXCEPTION to the general 'avoid calcium in TLS' rule. This client meets BOTH criteria for calcium use: (1) hyperkalemic ECG changes (peaked T's, widened QRS) and (2) symptomatic hypocalcemia (Chvostek, Trousseau, perioral numbness, PVCs). Calcium does NOT lower K+ but stabilizes the cardiac membrane while other interventions shift and remove K+.

opts.text: Regular insulin 10 units IV PUSH + D50W 25 g IV PUSH simultaneously
opts.rat: Drives K+ intracellularly within minutes. Insulin activates Na/K-ATPase pump moving K+ into cells. D50W prevents the inevitable hypoglycemia from insulin. Effect lasts ~4–6 hours — bridges to definitive K+ elimination by Kayexalate or dialysis.

opts.text: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) 30 g PO or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO
opts.rat: Removes K+ from the body by exchanging in the GI tract. Onset is hours (slower than insulin shift), but is one of the few interventions that actually eliminates K+ rather than just redistributing it. Sodium zirconium is preferred when available — fewer GI side effects.

opts.text: Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV x1 after verifying G6PD status
opts.rat: Urgent uric acid lowering for established TLS with AKI. Rasburicase enzymatically converts uric acid to water-soluble allantoin within hours, vs allopurinol which takes days. ⚠️ ABSOLUTELY contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (causes severe hemolysis and methemoglobinemia). Screen before administration.

opts.text: Increase 0.9% NS hydration to 250 mL/hr to maintain UOP >100 mL/hr
opts.rat: Cornerstone of TLS management. Aggressive hydration dilutes the precipitable solutes (uric acid, phosphate) and maintains urine flow to flush them out. Target UOP ~2 mL/kg/hr. 0.9% NS preferred over LR (LR contains K+).

opts.text: Aluminum hydroxide 30 mL PO q4h with meals as a phosphate binder
opts.rat: Binds dietary phosphate in the GI tract, preventing absorption. Helps lower serum phosphate over time, which secondarily improves calcium and reduces calcium-phosphate precipitation risk. Sevelamer is an alternative.

opts.text: Nephrology consult for emergent CRRT preparation
opts.rat: Established clinical TLS with AKI may require renal replacement therapy if medical management doesn't rapidly correct derangements. CRRT (continuous) is preferred over intermittent HD in unstable clients with massive ongoing solute load. Don't wait for failure — consult early.

opts.text: Routine IV calcium gluconate replacement for the asymptomatic hypocalcemia component
opts.rat: ⚠️ HIGH-YIELD NCLEX TRAP. Calcium replacement in TLS pushes the equilibrium toward calcium-phosphate precipitation, accelerating AKI. Reserve calcium ONLY for: (1) symptomatic tetany/seizures, OR (2) hyperkalemic ECG changes (membrane stabilization). For asymptomatic hypocalcemia in TLS, treat the phosphate first; calcium will normalize.

opts.text: Furosemide 40 mg IV push to 'flush out the uric acid'
opts.rat: Not first-line in TLS. Risks volume depletion that would concentrate urine and worsen urate precipitation. Aggressive hydration is the volume strategy; loop diuretic is reserved only for volume overload from oliguric AKI with adequate intravascular volume.

opts.text: Sodium bicarbonate IV drip to alkalinize the urine and prevent uric acid crystallization
opts.rat: Outdated practice. Modern guidelines recommend AGAINST routine alkalinization in TLS because it shifts the equilibrium toward calcium-phosphate precipitation, worsening AKI. Reserve bicarbonate for severe metabolic acidosis with pH <7.20 and hemodynamic compromise.

opts.text: Lactated Ringers IV at 250 mL/hr as the hydration fluid
opts.rat: INCORRECT in hyperkalemia. LR contains 4 mEq/L of potassium — would worsen the very problem being treated. Always use 0.9% NS or other potassium-free crystalloid in TLS.

opts.text: Continue allopurinol 300 mg PO daily as the sole xanthine-oxidase agent for established TLS with AKI
opts.rat: Insufficient for established TLS. Allopurinol prevents NEW uric acid formation but does not catabolize existing uric acid; takes days for effect. For established TLS with AKI, rasburicase is preferred. Allopurinol may be continued concurrently or after rasburicase to prevent rebound.

opts.text: Neutropenic precautions: private room, mask for visitors, no fresh flowers, no fresh fruits or vegetables, meticulous hand hygiene
opts.rat: Essential given ANC 120. Reduces exposure to environmental pathogens. Fresh produce harbors gram-negative bacteria (Pseudomonas, etc.); fresh flowers and standing water can carry mold spores (Aspergillus, etc.). Strict hand hygiene from all staff is the highest-yield infection prevention measure.

opts.text: Encourage potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, leafy greens) for the muscle cramps
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS. Muscle cramps in this client are from hypocalcemia, NOT hypokalemia. K+ is already critically HIGH. Dietary potassium must be RESTRICTED in hyperkalemia, not encouraged.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Call rapid response / oncology / nephrology STAT; mobilize ICU transfer team; do not leave the client
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Clinical TLS with AKI and arrhythmia requires ICU-level care and multidisciplinary management. Calling for help mobilizes the team needed to execute the parallel interventions of K+ shift, rasburicase, hydration, and possible CRRT.

opts.text: Confirm IV access patent; administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly over 10 minutes for cardiac membrane stabilization
opts.rat: #2 — Immediate. Cardiac membrane stabilization is the FIRST step in hyperkalemia with ECG changes because it protects against arrhythmia while slower-acting interventions (insulin shift, Kayexalate, dialysis) take effect. Acts within 1–3 minutes; effect lasts 30–60 minutes. Indicated here despite the general 'avoid calcium' rule because of symptomatic hypocalcemia + arrhythmia.

opts.text: Administer regular insulin 10 units IV PUSH + D50W 25 g IV PUSH simultaneously; add nebulized albuterol
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Shifts K+ intracellularly within minutes; effect lasts hours. Bridges the gap between membrane stabilization (calcium) and definitive K+ elimination (Kayexalate, dialysis). Albuterol provides additional shift via beta-2 receptor activation.

opts.text: Increase 0.9% NS to 250 mL/hr; administer rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV after verifying G6PD status
opts.rat: #4 — Addresses urate nephropathy and AKI. Rasburicase produces rapid uric acid clearance within hours. Aggressive hydration maintains UOP and prevents further crystal precipitation. G6PD verification is critical to prevent severe hemolysis.

opts.text: Administer aluminum hydroxide PO as phosphate binder; send repeat BMP/phosphorus/calcium/uric acid in 4 hours; prepare for possible CRRT if no improvement
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing management. Phosphate control reduces ongoing calcium-phosphate precipitation. Frequent lab monitoring tracks the response trajectory. CRRT preparation ensures readiness if medical management fails to correct derangements within hours.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 6 hours post-treatment: K+ 5.2, phosphorus 6.0, uric acid 1.4, ionized calcium 1.06, creatinine 2.0, UOP 110 mL/hr, PVCs resolved, ECG with normalized T-waves
opts.rat: Excellent response across all four metabolic derangements. Rasburicase produced near-complete uric acid clearance (typical 4–8 hour effect). K+ shift and elimination strategies effective. UOP recovery indicates renal function improving. Continue current management with q4h labs.

opts.text: Day 2: BMP near normal, BUN/Cr trending down, mental status clear, eating breakfast, family present and engaged; no progression to dialysis
opts.rat: Successful medical management of TLS without need for renal replacement therapy. Continue tumor-directed therapy with appropriate prophylaxis going forward. Document the lesson: this client should have received rasburicase prophylactically given high TLS risk on admission.

opts.text: Day 5: ANC recovering to 480, no fevers, neutropenic precautions continuing but mobilizing well; transitioned to oral allopurinol for continued uric acid prophylaxis through remaining induction days
opts.rat: Bone marrow recovery beginning (G-CSF support). Ongoing oral allopurinol prevents rebound hyperuricemia as lysis continues at lower rate. Will transition off when chemo cycle complete and counts recover.

opts.text: K+ climbs to 7.2 mEq/L despite shift therapy; develops sustained ventricular tachycardia; nephrology emergently initiates CRRT
opts.rat: Refractory hyperkalemia requiring renal replacement. CRRT removes K+ definitively. The progression to sustained VT warrants concurrent ACLS preparation. CRRT is preferred over intermittent HD in hemodynamically unstable clients.

opts.text: Anuric for 6 hours despite aggressive hydration; creatinine climbs to 4.8; emergent CRRT initiated
opts.rat: Progression to anuric AKI requiring renal replacement therapy. The kidneys can no longer clear the ongoing metabolic load. CRRT is initiated; tumor-directed therapy may need to continue concurrently to control the underlying lysis. Prognosis is worse but not hopeless.

opts.text: Sudden generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 45 seconds; ionized calcium drawn after seizure is 0.78
opts.rat: Severe symptomatic hypocalcemia precipitated seizure. Administer additional IV calcium gluconate emergently AND treat the underlying phosphate elevation. Seizure precautions, IV benzodiazepine if recurrent. Reflects the difficulty of metabolic management — calcium replacement is now clearly indicated again because of severe symptomatic hypocalcemia.

opts.text: Develops fever 102.4°F with rigors; new tachycardia 132 and hypotension 86/52; concern for septic shock
opts.rat: Febrile neutropenia with shock physiology. EMERGENCY. Draw blood cultures x2 from peripheral sites and from each Hickman lumen; start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (cefepime ± vancomycin) within 1 hour; aggressive crystalloid resuscitation; vasopressor support if needed. Neutropenic sepsis has very high mortality without rapid recognition and intervention.

opts.text: Rasburicase administered before G6PD result available; client develops dark urine, jaundice, Hgb drops from 9.2 to 6.4, cyanosis with SpO2 reading 88% despite supplemental O2
opts.rat: G6PD deficiency with rasburicase-induced acute hemolytic anemia AND methemoglobinemia. ⚠️ AVOIDABLE complication — G6PD status MUST be verified before rasburicase administration. Management: stop rasburicase, transfuse PRBCs, methylene blue is the antidote for methemoglobinemia BUT is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency, so ascorbic acid or hyperbaric oxygen are alternatives. Serious teaching moment.

opts.text: Mild residual fatigue, slow appetite recovery; petechiae stable; platelets stable at 35 K/µL; no active bleeding
opts.rat: Expected during induction recovery. Platelet recovery typically takes 2–3 weeks post-induction. Continue bleeding precautions, transfuse if platelets drop below 10 K/µL prophylactically or with active bleeding. Fatigue is universal during induction.

opts.text: Continued need for IV hydration and frequent labs over the next 24–48 hours as the cell lysis burden gradually resolves
opts.rat: Expected course. TLS risk typically extends 5–7 days post-chemo for high-burden disease as cells continue to die. Continue prophylactic allopurinol, monitor labs at decreasing frequency, advance diet as tolerated.
